hub_subtitle: Business English para as reuniões com o time Global da Sanofi, congressos médicos e a apresentação de agosto

lesson.menu_title: Handling Questions in Real Time -- Should, Could, Might When Global Asks the Unexpected
lesson.menu_desc: A Global faz a pergunta que voce nao esperava. A aula de PENSAR EM INGLES sob pressao: should (recomendacao), could (alternativa), might (possibilidade) -- e a frase que ganha tempo, 'That's a great question -- let me think about that for a second.' Ler a estrategia de uma boa resposta e depois responder ao vivo, com calma. -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 20 -- Handling Questions (should, could, might)
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Emmanuele Orrico | Lesson 20 -- Handling Questions (should, could, might)
lesson.grammar_point: modal verbs should, could, might (recommendation, possibility, uncertainty)

listenings.text: Hi Emmanuele, it's David from the Global team. A quick word before Thursday's review. On the call, people will ask you questions you cannot fully predict. That is normal. My advice is simple. Do not rush. If a question is hard, buy a second: say, that's a great question, let me think about that for a moment. Then answer with a modal. If you are recommending, use should: we should focus on the private market. If you are offering an option, use could: we could adjust the targeting. And if you are not sure, use might: the rollout might take a little longer. Nobody expects perfect answers. They want a calm, clear approach. You have got this. See you Thursday.

Hi Emmanuele, it's Claire. I listened to your practice Q and A, and I have a few comments. First, the good news. When you did not know an answer, you stayed calm and you stayed in English. That is a big win. Second, a small recommendation. You should slow down at the start of each answer. A short pause is not a weakness. It shows you are thinking. Third, use the three words we practiced. Should for your recommendation, could for an alternative, and might when there is uncertainty. One more idea. If a question is really hard, it is fine to say, one option could be this, and another might be that. You do not need one perfect answer. You need a clear approach. Well done, and talk soon.

extra_audio.text: That's a great question. Let me think about that for a second. I would say we should focus on the private market first. One option could be to adjust the HCP targeting. But there is some uncertainty, so the timeline might change a little. My recommendation is to start small and grow.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: the chance that something bad could happen
advice about the best thing to do
a different option you can choose
a way of doing or dealing with something
the feeling of not being sure what will happen
something that might happen or might be true
an idea you offer for someone to think about

paras: Every quarter, Emmanuele presents Brazil's results to the Global team. She prepares the slides for days, and she knows the numbers well. But there is one part she cannot prepare: the questions. After the presentation, someone from Global always asks something she did not expect. "What is the biggest risk right now?" "What should Brazil do differently in Q4?" "What could go wrong with the launch?" For a long time, these questions were her nightmare. Her mind went blank, and she switched to Portuguese.

Then a mentor gave her a simple idea: the answer is not the hard part -- the first second is. When a hard question comes, do not rush. Buy one second. Say, "That's a great question -- let me think about that for a second." That short pause is not a weakness. It shows you are thinking, and it gives your brain time to find the words. A calm approach is more professional than a fast, nervous one.

And there are three small words that make any answer sound clear and confident. Use should for a recommendation: "We should focus on the private market." Use could for an alternative or a possibility: "We could adjust the targeting," or "One option could be to start in the Northeast." And use might when there is uncertainty: "The rollout might take a little longer." These are modal verbs, and they all work the same way: the modal plus the base verb -- should focus, could adjust, might take. No "to", and no extra "s".

So now, when Global asks the unexpected, Emmanuele has a plan. She buys a second, she stays in English, and she answers with should, could, or might. She does not need a perfect answer. She needs a clear approach and a calm voice. "I don't know" becomes "That's a great question -- one option could be this, and another might be that." And that, in any language, is what confidence sounds like.

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: A good presenter must answer every question instantly, with no pause.
When Global asks an unexpected question, buy a second to think and answer calmly with should, could, or might.
The safest thing to do with a hard question is to switch to Portuguese.

items: The text says the hardest part of the presentation is the questions Emmanuele cannot prepare.
t
"there is one part she cannot prepare: the questions."
According to the text, a short pause before answering is a sign of weakness.
f
The opposite: "That short pause is not a weakness. It shows you are thinking."
The text says you use should for a recommendation.
t
"Use should for a recommendation: We should focus on the private market."
In the text, might is the word for when you are sure about something.
f
Might is for UNCERTAINTY -- when you are not sure: "The rollout might take a little longer."

about that for a second. I think we
focus on the private market first -- that is my recommendation. We
also adjust the HCP targeting; that is one option. There is still some

gapfill.text: The three modal verbs work the same way: modal + the base verb, with no "to" and no extra "s". SHOULD = a recommendation, the best idea: "We should focus on the private market." COULD = an alternative or a polite option: "We could adjust the targeting," "One option could be to start small." MIGHT = uncertainty, something possible but not sure: "The rollout might take longer." Say should FOCUS (not "should to focus", not "should focuses"), could ADJUST, might TAKE. And to buy time before a hard answer: "That's a great question -- let me think about that for a second."

bank.label: Useful language for handling questions in real time
items: That's a great question -- let me think about that for a second.
My recommendation is that we should focus on the private market.
One option could be to adjust the HCP targeting.
I would suggest we start small and grow.
There is some uncertainty, so the timeline might change.
I would rather start in the Northeast than wait.
That's a good point -- let me come back to you on that.

lf.title: Which is it -- a recommendation, an option, or uncertainty?
items: A
We should focus on the private market.
-- a recommendation (should)
strong
B
We could adjust the HCP targeting.
-- an alternative / option (could)
soft
C
The rollout might take a little longer.
-- uncertainty (might)
soft
D
My recommendation is that we should start small.
-- a recommendation (should)
strong

lf.text: SHOULD (A and D) gives your recommendation -- the best idea, what you advise. COULD (B) offers an alternative or a polite option -- one possible way, not the only way. MIGHT (C) shows uncertainty -- something possible, but you are not sure. In a real Q and A, you mix all three: "We should focus on the private market (recommendation). We could adjust the targeting (option). And the timeline might change a little (uncertainty)."

items.situation: David asks: "What should Brazil focus on in Q4?"
tips: We should focus on the private market first.
My recommendation is that we should protect the top accounts.

items.situation: "What could go wrong with the launch?"
tips: The approval could be late, so we could start with the private market.
One risk is the supply; we could keep a backup stock.

items.situation: "Are you sure about the Q4 timeline?"
tips: There is some uncertainty, so it might change a little.
It might take a bit longer, but the plan is on track.

items.situation: "What is the biggest obstacle right now?"
tips: The main obstacle is access in the private market.
That's a great question -- one obstacle could be the approval timeline.

items.situation: A very hard question you did not expect.
tips: That's a great question -- let me think about that for a second.
That's a good point -- let me come back to you on that.

items.situation: "Do you have an alternative if the plan does not work?"
tips: Yes -- one alternative could be to start in the Northeast.
We could adjust the approach and focus on fewer regions.
